sections.text: Welcome to the final module of AP Computer Science A. Module Ten — recursion. This is the unit that intimidates students more than any other on the exam. By the end of this lesson you will be able to read a recursive method, trace its call stack, and write one yourself. Take your time. Recursion is a way of thinking, not just a syntax.

Picture a matryoshka doll. A painted Russian nesting doll. You open it, and inside is a smaller doll. You open that one, and inside is an even smaller doll. You keep going until you reach the tiniest doll, the one that does not open. Recursion is exactly that. A method that calls itself, with a slightly smaller problem each time, until it hits the smallest case it can answer directly.

Five things to cover. One — what recursion actually is. Two — the two required parts every recursive method must have. Three — three classic examples: factorial, Fibonacci, and sum of digits. Four — binary search, a real-world place recursion shines. Five — when to use it, and the risks. This is the hardest module in the course. Slow down.

Recursion is a method that calls itself. That is the whole idea. It works because each time the method calls itself, it does so with a smaller version of the problem — smaller input, simpler case. Eventually the input becomes so small that the answer is obvious, and we return that answer directly. Then all the earlier calls finish, one after another.

Every recursive method must have two parts. One — a base case. The smallest sub-problem, the one we answer without calling the method again. Two — a recursive case. The part where the method calls itself, but on a smaller input. Miss the base case, or write a base case that never triggers, and your program will recurse forever and crash with a StackOverflowError. Both parts. Every time.

The classic example. Factorial. n factorial equals n times n minus one factorial. In Java, public int fact of int n. If n equals zero, return one — that is our base case. Otherwise, return n times fact of n minus one. That is the recursive call. Two lines of real logic. Watch how it works on a concrete number next.

Let us trace fact of four. fact of four asks for four times fact of three. fact of three asks for three times fact of two. fact of two asks for two times fact of one. fact of one asks for one times fact of zero. fact of zero hits the base case and returns one. Now the stack unwinds. One. Then one. Then two. Then six. Then twenty-four. The call stack went down, hit the bottom, then came back up multiplying as it returned.

Fibonacci. Each number is the sum of the two before it. Zero, one, one, two, three, five, eight, thirteen. In code, public int fib of int n. If n is less than or equal to one, return n — two base cases handled at once. Otherwise, return fib of n minus one plus fib of n minus two. Elegant. But warning — it is exponentially slow for large n, because it recomputes the same values many times. We will fix that in a future course.

Your turn. Write sumOfDigits of int n recursively. For example, sumOfDigits of one-two-three should return six — one plus two plus three. Hint. Your base case is when n is less than ten — a single digit returns itself. Your recursive case takes the last digit, n mod ten, plus a recursive call on n divided by ten. Pause now. Solve it. Press play when you are ready.

Time. Here is the answer. public int sumOfDigits of int n. If n is less than ten, return n — base case. Otherwise, return n mod ten plus sumOfDigits of n divided by ten. Trace one-two-three. Three plus sumOfDigits of twelve. That becomes three plus two plus sumOfDigits of one. That becomes three plus two plus one. Six. The recursion peels off the last digit each time until only one digit remains.

Recursion is not just for numbers. Watch this — reverse a String. If the String length is one or zero, return it as is — that is the base case. Otherwise, return rev of s dot substring of one, plus s dot charAt of zero. We peel off the first character, reverse the rest, and stick the first character on the end. The string shrinks by one character each call. Beautiful when you see it.

Here is recursion doing real work. Binary search. Imagine a sorted array, and we are looking for seventeen. Pick the middle element. If it is seventeen — done. If seventeen is smaller than the middle, recurse on the left half. If seventeen is larger, recurse on the right half. Each call cuts the search space in half. That is order log n. Searching a million items takes about twenty steps. This is why recursion matters.

The big danger. StackOverflowError. On the left — wrong. A method that calls itself with no base case. It will recurse forever, the call stack fills up, the program crashes. On the right — right. Check the base case first, before any recursive call. Always check base first. Always recurse with a smaller input. Two rules. Break either one, infinite recursion.

When should you reach for recursion? When the problem has a naturally recursive structure. Tree and graph traversal. Divide-and-conquer algorithms like binary search and merge sort. Parsing nested structures — file systems, JSON, expression trees. For a simple counting loop, iteration is clearer and faster. Recursion is a tool. Match it to the problem shape.

Recursion versus iteration. On the left — recursion. Cleaner for tree-like and divide-and-conquer problems. Uses stack memory, one frame per call. Can be slow if written naively, like our Fibonacci. On the right — iteration, using for and while loops. Cleaner for simple counted loops. Constant memory. Harder to express for tree problems. Both are valid. Choose the one the problem asks for.

The three most common mistakes. One — forgetting the base case entirely. Two — writing a base case the recursion never reaches, because the input is not actually shrinking. Three — calling the method with the same or larger input, so you never make progress. Fix is always the same. Check the base case first. Recurse on a strictly smaller input. Verify by hand on a small example.

Recap. Recursion is a method that calls itself. Every recursive method needs a base case and a recursive case. Factorial, Fibonacci, sum of digits, string reverse, and binary search are the classics — memorize the shape of each. Trace by drawing the call stack going down and coming back up. Use recursion for tree and divide-and-conquer problems. For simple loops, iteration is usually clearer. Your assignment is in the dashboard.

Reminder — this video is the overview. Recursion clicks only after you have written ten or fifteen tiny recursive methods yourself. Read the Recursion chapter on Runestone — its visualizer shows the call stack live. Then do Codingbat Recursion-one and Recursion-two — fifteen problems total. Submit your assignment in the dashboard. Book a check-in with your advisor. And one more thing — you have just finished AP Computer Science A. Massive accomplishment. Talk to your advisor about practice exams next.
